ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (noodle-based dish with meat) is the closest defensible parent; no specific Bun Cha code exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Bun Cha Ha Noi or a sufficiently close Vietnamese noodle dish category.

serving.description: 1 full set (noodles, grilled pork belly, pork meatballs, dipping sauce, pickled vegetables, spring roll, herbs)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Bun Cha Ha Noi is approximately 450 g, comprising a bowl of rice vermicelli noodles, grilled pork belly slices, grilled pork meatballs in fish sauce dipping broth, pickled green papaya and carrot, one or two crispy spring rolls, and a side of fresh herbs and vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Bun Cha (Vietnamese grilled pork noodle dish) — component-based estimation using FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for individual ingredients
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Food Composition Table; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Bun Cha Ha Noi as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from weighted component analysis: rice vermicelli (~35% by weight), grilled pork belly (~25%), pork meatballs (~15%), fish sauce dipping broth (~10%), pickled vegetables (~8%), spring roll (~5%), herbs and garnish (~2%). Primary references: FAO/INFOODS SEA Table (rice vermicelli, pork, vegetables) and USDA SR Legacy (pork belly, ground pork, rice noodles). Values represent a plausible mid-range estimate for a typical restaurant serving normalised to 100 g.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice vermicelli and mixed pork noodle dish analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K et al. (2021) American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bun Cha Ha Noi. GI of 53 is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate vehicle: rice vermicelli noodles (GI ~53, low category, per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables). The protein and fat content of the dish further attenuate the glycemic response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (53 × 66.6 g carbohydrate in 450 g serving) / 100 ≈ 35.3, rounded to 35.5.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce dipping broth and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Bun Cha Ha Noi due to its high sodium content (approximately 520 mg per 100 g, equating to ~2340 mg per standard 450 g serving), which approaches or exceeds the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; requesting reduced fish sauce or consuming only a portion of the dipping broth can substantially lower sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice vermicelli noodles, partially offset by protein and fat content
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that a full serving of Bun Cha Ha Noi carries a moderate-to-high glycemic load (~35 GL units per 450 g serving) driven by rice vermicelli; portion control, increasing the proportion of herbs and vegetables, and reducing noodle quantity can help moderate postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from grilled pork belly and cholesterol content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate consumption of Bun Cha Ha Noi due to its saturated fat content (~2.1 g per 100 g) from pork belly and the deep-fried spring roll component; choosing leaner pork cuts, omitting the spring roll, and limiting the fish sauce broth can reduce saturated fat, cholesterol, and sodium burden on the cardiovascular system.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bun Cha Ha Noi (Bún chả Hà Nội) is a classic Vietnamese noodle dish originating from Hanoi, northern Vietnam. It consists of grilled pork belly slices and grilled pork meatballs served in a warm, slightly sweet and tangy fish sauce dipping broth, accompanied by a separate plate of white rice vermicelli noodles, fresh herbs (perilla, mint, coriander), bean sprouts, and pickled green papaya and carrot. A crispy deep-fried spring roll is often included as a side component. The dish is assembled by the diner, who dips noodles and herbs into the broth. The flavour profile is savoury, smoky from the charcoal-grilled pork, lightly sweet from sugar in the dipping sauce, and bright from lime and fresh herbs. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-energy mixed meal providing approximately 148 kcal per 100 g, with meaningful protein (~9.2 g/100 g) from pork, moderate carbohydrate (~14.8 g/100 g) from rice vermicelli, and notable sodium (~520 mg/100 g) from fish sauce. The glycemic index is estimated at 53 (low category) based on rice vermicelli as the dominant carbohydrate vehicle, though the glycemic load per full serving is moderate to high. Clinically, the primary concern is sodium for hypertensive individuals; the dish is suitable in moderation for most adults but requires portion awareness for those managing blood glucose or cardiovascular risk. Bun Cha Ha Noi is widely served in Vietnamese restaurants across Singapore and Southeast Asia.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Bun Cha Ha Noi as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from weighted component analysis using FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA SR Legacy data for individual ingredients. GI is estimated from the rice vermicelli analogue in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved databases and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-based estimates normalised to 100 g; actual restaurant values may vary depending on pork fat content, fish sauce quantity, and spring roll inclusion. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA SR Legacy for this dish type. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used for this estimation. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in FAO/INFOODS SEA for the relevant components. 5. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy pork belly and ground pork profiles. 6. The cuisine field is set to 'Vietnamese' reflecting the dish's true origin; the input record listed 'Singapore' as cuisine, which reflects the serving context (field survey data) rather than culinary origin. 7. The food_name has been normalised from 'Bun Cha Ha Noi - BEST SELLING' to the canonical form 'Bun Cha Ha Noi', removing the promotional suffix. 8. Halal status is marked false as traditional Bun Cha uses pork; halal-certified variants may exist but cannot be assumed. 9. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (53 × 66.6 g carbohydrate in 450 g serving) / 100 ≈ 35.5.
